sections.question: 1. Pursuant to Sections 82002 and 82032 and 82037, is the farmer attempting to influence legislative and administrative action? In answering this question, particular attention should be paid to the attached copy of Chapter 146. 2. The attorney will become a lobbyist if he seeks to influence legislative and administrative action for 40 hours, 10 of which are in direct communication, in a two-month period. Regulation 18239(b) states that direct communication includes correspondence. In this case the correspondence is from the farmer but it was prepared by the attorney. The comment to 18239(e) states that background research is not direct communication unless it is presented directly to the legislative or administrative body. This comment would suggest that the time spent preparing the letter for the farmer should be counted as direct communication by the attorney. From a policy standpoint, it seems absurd to conclude that this attorney might become a lobbyist merely by drafting letters under another's signature. What do you think was intended?
sections.question_synthetic: Does a farmer seeking to communicate with the ALRB Legislative Oversight Committee constitute attempting to influence legislative and administrative action, and does an attorney preparing correspondence for the farmer's signature count as direct communication that could trigger lobbyist registration requirements?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request memorandum to FPPC opinion request meeting participants, not an FPPC response letter. The document lacks formal Q/A sections but contains numbered questions. Document quality is degraded with OCR errors (e.g., 'Memorand', 'stion', 'Legis', 'Overs'). No FPPC conclusion is present. TP/jo notation at end appears to be reference initials.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1. Pursuant to Sections 82002 and 82032 and 82037, is the farmer attempting to influence legislative and administrative action? In answering this question, particular attention should be paid to the attached copy of Chapter 146. 2. The attorney will become a lobbyist if he seeks to influence legislative and administrative action for 40 hours, 10 of which are in direct communication, in a two-month period. Regulation 18239(b) states that direct communication includes correspondence. In this case the correspondence is from the farmer but it was prepared by the attorney. The comment to 18239(e) states that background research is not direct communication unless it is presented directly to the legislative or administrative body. This comment would suggest that the time spent preparing the letter for the farmer should be counted as direct communication by the attorney. From a policy standpoint, it seems absurd to conclude that this attorney might become a lobbyist merely by drafting letters under another's signature. What do you think was intended?

embedding.summary: A Fresno law firm submitted an opinion request regarding whether a farmer and his retained attorney would be subject to lobbying disclosure requirements when the attorney drafts a letter to the ALRB Legislative Oversight Committee for the farmer's signature and testifies on the farmer's behalf.
